2 (6) To receive funding under this section, a regional data hub
3 must have a governance model that ensures local control of data,
4 data security, and student privacy issues. The integration of data
5 within each of the regional data hubs must provide for the
6 actionable use of data by districts and intermediate districts
7 through common reports and dashboards and for efficiently providing
8 information to meet state and federal reporting purposes.
9 (7) Participation in a data hub region in the Michigan data
10 hub network under this section is voluntary and is not required.
11 (8) Entities receiving funding under this section shall use
12 the funds for all of the following:
13 (a) Creating an infrastructure that effectively manages the
14 movement of data between data systems used by intermediate
15 districts, districts, and other educational organizations in
16 Michigan based on common data standards to improve student
17 achievement.
18 (b) Utilizing the infrastructure to put in place commonly
19 needed integrations, reducing cost and effort to do that work while
20 increasing data accuracy and usability.
21 (c) Promoting the use of a more common set of applications by
22 promoting systems that integrate with the Michigan data hub
23 network.
24 (d) Promoting 100% district adoption of the Michigan data hub
25 network.
26 (e) Ensuring local control of data, data security, and student
27 data privacy.
28 (f) Utilizing the infrastructure to promote the actionable use
29 of data through common reports and dashboards that are consistent
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

1 statewide.
2 (g) Creating a governance model to facilitate sustainable

7 determine whether the initiatives can be enhanced by using the

93
1 (i) Outcomes that put pupils on track to meet or exceed grade
2 level proficiency and that are based on district or public school
3 academy needs identified as required under section 21h.

6 assessments described in section 104b or 104c.
7 (B) At least 1 proficiency or growth outcome based on a
8 benchmark assessment described in section 104h.

10 graduation rates, as applicable.
11 (iv) Outcomes that measure attendance rates.
12 (b) Accountability measures to be imposed if the district or

14 outcomes described in subdivision (a) or section 21h(2)(b) for each
15 school operated by the district or public school academy that is
16 subject to the partnership district agreement. For a district
17 assigned as a partnership district as described in this subsection,
18 accountability measures under this subdivision must include the
19 reconstitution of the school. For a public school academy assigned
20 as a partnership district as described in this subsection,
21 accountability measures under this subdivision may include the
22 reconstitution of the school.
23 (c) For a public school academy assigned as a partnership
24 district as described in this subsection, a requirement that, if
25 reconstitution is imposed on a school that is operated by the
26 public school academy and that is subject to the partnership
27 district agreement, the school must be reconstituted as described
28 in section 507, 528, or 561, as applicable, of the revised school
29 code, MCL 380.507, 380.528, and 380.561.
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

94
1 (d) For a district assigned as a partnership district as
2 described in this subsection, a provision that, if reconstitution

21 health and human services to reside in or to attend a juvenile
22 detention facility or child caring institution licensed by the
23 department of health and human services or the department of
24 licensing and regulatory affairs and approved by the department to

27 described in this section from total costs, as approved by the
28 department, in whole or in part, for educating those pupils in the
29 on-grounds education program or in a program approved by the

1 department that is located on property adjacent to a juvenile

6 total amount allocated under this section for a fiscal year by the
7 full-time equated membership total for all pupils approved by the
8 department to be funded under this section for that fiscal year for
9 the district or intermediate district.

5 Sec. 29. (1) The enrollment stabilization fund is created as a
6 separate account in the state school aid fund for the purpose of
7 stabilizing the effects of declining enrollment.
8 (2) The state treasurer may receive money or other assets from
9 any source for deposit into the enrollment stabilization fund. The
10 state treasurer shall direct the investment of the enrollment
11 stabilization fund. The state treasurer shall credit to the
12 enrollment stabilization fund interest and earnings from enrollment
13 stabilization fund investments.
14 (3) Money in the enrollment stabilization fund at the close of
15 the fiscal year remains in the enrollment stabilization fund and
16 does not lapse to the state school aid fund or the general fund.
17 (4) The department of treasury is the administrator of the
18 enrollment stabilization fund for auditing purposes.
19 (5) Money available in the enrollment stabilization fund must
20 not be expended without a specific appropriation.
21 (6) From the enrollment stabilization fund money appropriated
22 under section 11, there is allocated an amount not to exceed

4 center not later than the fifth Wednesday after the pupil

10 or public school academy, as reported to the center in the form and
11 manner prescribed by the center not later than the fifth Wednesday

4 (4) Each district or public school academy must be assigned an
5 opportunity index score each fiscal year, the value of which is the
6 quotient of the number of economically disadvantaged pupils as
7 determined under subsection (3) for the district or public school
8 academy and the total number of pupils in the district or public
9 school academy in the immediately preceding fiscal year, multiplied
10 by 100 and rounded up to the nearest whole number. Each district or
11 public school academy must be assigned an opportunity index band as
12 follows:
13 (a) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
14 index score greater than or equal to 0 but less than 20 must be
15 assigned to band 1 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
16 (3) at a rate of at least 35.0% and less than 36.0%. The
17 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
18 to the district's opportunity index score minus 1, multiplied by
19 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
20 35.0%.
21 (b) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
22 index score greater than or equal to 20 but less than 44 must be
23 assigned to band 2 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
24 (3) at a rate of at least 36.0% and less than 37.5%. The
25 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
26 to the district's opportunity index score minus 20, multiplied by
27 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
28 36.0%.
29 (c) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

133
1 index score greater than or equal to 44 but less than 59 must be
2 assigned to band 3 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
3 (3) at a rate of at least 37.5% and less than 39.0%. The
4 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
5 to the district's opportunity index score minus 44, multiplied by
6 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
7 37.5%.
8 (d) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
9 index score greater than or equal to 59 but less than 73 must be
10 assigned to band 4 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
11 (3) at a rate of at least 39.0% and less than 42.0%. The
12 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
13 to the district's opportunity index score minus 59, multiplied by
14 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
15 39.0%.
16 (e) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
17 index score greater than or equal to 73 but less than 85 must be
18 assigned to band 5 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
19 (3) at a rate of at least 42.0% and less than 47.0%. The
20 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
21 to the district's opportunity index score minus 73, multiplied by
22 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
23 42.0%.
24 (f) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
25 index score greater than or equal to 85 must be assigned to band 6
26 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection (3) at a rate of
27 47.0%.
28 (g) As used in this subsection, "band adjustment factor" means
29 an amount equal to the difference between the lowest and highest
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

134
1 reimbursement bounds for each band, divided by the number of
2 possible opportunity index scores in that band.

14 (a) "At-risk pupil" means a pupil in grades pre-K to 12 for
15 whom the district has documentation that the pupil meets any of the
16 following criteria:
17 (i) The pupil is economically disadvantaged.
18 (ii) The pupil is an English language learner.
19 (iii) The pupil is chronically absent as defined by and reported
20 to the center.
21 (iv) The pupil is a victim of child abuse or neglect.
22 (v) The pupil is a pregnant teenager or teenage parent.
23 (vi) The pupil has a family history of school failure,
24 incarceration, or substance abuse.
25 (vii) The pupil is an immigrant who has immigrated within the
26 immediately preceding 3 years.
27 (viii) The pupil did not complete high school in 4 years and is
28 still continuing in school as identified in the Michigan cohort
29 graduation and dropout report.

1 (ix) For pupils for whom the results of the state summative

6 public school academy's core academic curricular objectives in
7 English language arts or mathematics, as demonstrated on local
8 assessments.
9 (b) "Economically disadvantaged" means a pupil who has been
10 determined eligible for free or reduced-price meals as determined
11 under the Richard B. Russell national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751

19 understanding English as reported to the center.

4 approved by the department, less federal reimbursement, participant
5 payments, and state breakfast reimbursements received under section
6 30d. The department shall determine the statewide average cost
7 using costs as reported in a manner approved by the department for
8 the preceding school year.
9 (4) Notwithstanding section 17b, the department may make
10 payments under this section pursuant to an agreement with the
11 department.
12 (5) In purchasing food for a school breakfast program funded
13 under this section, a district shall give preference to food that
14 is grown or produced by Michigan businesses if it is competitively
15 priced and of comparable quality.
16 Sec. 31j. (1) From the general fund money appropriated in
17 section 11, there is allocated for 2024-2025 2025-2026 an amount

20 exceed $4,000,000.00 for 2024-2025 2025-2026 for a program to

22 locally grown fruits, vegetables, and legumes as described in this
23 section.
24 (2) Funding under this section retained by the department for
25 administration must not exceed 5%. Funding under this section
26 retained by project partners for data collection, outreach, and
27 training must not exceed 2% for each partner.
28 (3) The department shall develop and implement a competitive
29 grant program for districts and other non-school sponsors to assist
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

149
1 in paying for the costs incurred by the district or other non-
2 school sponsor to purchase or increase purchases of whole or
3 minimally processed fruits, vegetables, and legumes grown in this
4 state. The maximum amount that may be drawn down on a grant to a
5 district or other non-school sponsor is based on the number of
6 meals served by the district during the previous school year under
7 the Richard B. Russell national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751 to
8 1769j, or meals served by the other non-school sponsor in the
9 previous school year. The department shall collaborate with the
10 Michigan department of agriculture and rural development to provide
11 training to newly participating schools and other non-school
12 sponsors and electronic information on Michigan agriculture.
13 (4) The goals of the program under this section include
14 improving daily nutrition and eating habits for children through
15 the school and child care settings while investing in Michigan's
16 agricultural and related food business economy.
17 (5) A district or other non-school sponsor that receives a
18 grant under this section shall use those funds for the costs
19 incurred by the district or the sponsor to do both of the
20 following:
21 (a) Purchase whole or minimally processed fruits, vegetables,
22 and legumes that meet both of the following:
23 (i) For each fiscal year, were purchased for use in meals and
24 supportive activities as part of the United States Department of
25 Agriculture child nutrition programs provided between October 1
26 through September 30 of that fiscal year.
27 (ii) Are grown in this state and, if minimally processed, are
28 also processed in this state.
29 (b) Pay for labor and food transportation of locally grown
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

150
1 fruits, vegetables, and legumes that directly support the
2 activities and goals of the program described in this section. The
3 amount paid under this subdivision must not exceed 25% of the grant
4 award.
5 (6) For Michigan-grown fruits, vegetables, and legumes that
6 satisfy the requirements of subsection (5), the department shall
7 make matching reimbursements in an amount not to exceed 10 cents
8 for every school meal that is served as part of the United States
9 Department of Agriculture's child nutrition programs.
10 (7) In awarding grants under this section, the department
11 shall work in consultation with Michigan-based farm to school
12 resource organizations, to develop scoring criteria that assess an
13 applicant's ability to procure Michigan-grown products, prepare and
14 menu Michigan-grown products, promote and market Michigan-grown
15 products, and submit letters of intent from districts or other non-
16 school sponsors on plans for educational activities that promote
17 the goals of the program.
18 (8) The department shall give preference to districts or other
19 non-school sponsors that propose educational activities that meet 1
20 or more of the following: promote healthy food activities; have
21 clear educational objectives; involve parents or the community;
22 connect to a school's or child care center's farm-to-school or
23 farm-to-early-child-care procurement activities; and market and
24 promote the program, leading to increased pupil knowledge and
25 consumption of Michigan-grown products. The department shall give
26 stronger weighting and consideration to applications with robust
27 marketing and promotional activities.
28 (9) In awarding grants, the department shall also consider all
29 of the following:

3 event, and must agree to comply with a comprehensive investigation.
4 All of the following apply to a comprehensive investigation
5 described in this subsection:
6 (a) The comprehensive investigation will assess the
7 circumstances surrounding the mass casualty event, including, but
8 not limited to:
9 (i) Emergency response effectiveness.
10 (ii) Compliance with safety protocols.
11 (iii) Communication procedures.
12 (iv) Any factors contributing to the incident.
13 (b) The governor shall designate an appropriate person or
14 investigative entity to conduct the comprehensive investigation.
15 This person or investigative entity may include, but is not limited
16 to, state law enforcement agencies, independent review boards, or
17 specially appointed task forces. The person or designated
18 investigative entity has the authority to do all of the following:
19 (i) Access relevant records and data from the district.
20 (ii) Interview witnesses and district personnel involved.
21 (iii) Issue findings and recommendations based on the
22 investigation.
23 (c) The person or investigative entity designated in
24 subdivision (b) shall prepare a detailed report of its findings and
25 submit the report to the governor and relevant legislative
26 committees within 90 days following the conclusion of the
27 investigation. The report must include recommendations for
28 preventing future incidents and improving school safety protocols.
29 (7) In addition to the funds allocated in subsection (1), from
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

18 as determined by the department of lifelong education, advancement,
19 and potential, are enrolled before children with lesser needs.

23 board, including, at least, the Connect4Learning curriculum.

8 facilities for other early care and education activities, in the
9 following order of priority:
10 (i) Early care and education activities under a federal award.
11 (ii) Early care and education activities under other state
12 awards.
13 (iii) Early care and education activities under local or
14 regional awards.

1 schedule length, as determined by the department of lifelong

27 teacher-child contact time per day but for fewer hours of teacher-
28 child contact time per day than a school-day program.

1 at least the same length of day as a district's first grade program

9 1(1)(i)(i) to (v) of 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111.
10 (d) "Licensed child care organization" means a child care
11 organization that has been issued a license under 1973 PA 116, MCL
12 722.111 to 722.128, to operate a child care organization.
13 (e) "School age" means a child who is eligible to attend a
14 grade of kindergarten or higher, but is less than 13 years of age.
15 A child is considered to be school age on the first day of the
16 school year in which the child is eligible under section 1 of 1973
17 PA 116, MCL 722.111.

20 at grade level by the end of grade 3.

1 (a) Be offered through a system of training that provides

1 (i) Team-based leadership.

7 (12) Notwithstanding section 17b, the department shall make

10 (13) As used in this section:
11 (a) "Dyslexia" means both of the following:
12 (i) A specific learning disorder that is neurobiological in
13 origin and characterized by difficulties with accurate or fluent
14 word recognition and by poor spelling and decoding abilities that
15 typically result from a deficit in the phonological component of
16 language that is often unexpected in relation to other cognitive
17 abilities and the provision of effective classroom instruction.
18 (ii) A specific learning disorder that may include secondary
19 consequences, such as problems in reading comprehension and a
20 reduced reading experience that can impede the growth of vocabulary
21 and background knowledge and lead to social, emotional, and
22 behavioral difficulties.
23 (b) "Evidence-based" means an activity, program, process,
24 service, strategy, or intervention that demonstrates statistically
25 significant effects on improving pupil outcomes or other relevant
26 outcomes and that meets at least both of the following:
27 (i) At least 1 of the following:
28 (A) Is based on strong evidence from at least 1 well-designed
29 and well-implemented experimental study.
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

229
1 (B) Is based on moderate evidence from at least 1 well-
2 designed and well-implemented quasi-experimental study.
3 (C) Is based on promising evidence from at least 1 well-
4 designed and well-implemented correlational study with statistical
5 controls for selection bias.
6 (D) Demonstrates a rationale based on high-quality research
7 findings or positive evaluation that the activity, program,
8 process, service, strategy, or intervention is likely to improve
9 pupil outcomes or other relevant outcomes.
10 (ii) Includes ongoing efforts to examine the effects of the
11 activity, program, process, service, strategy, or intervention.
12 (c) "Explicit" means direct and deliberate instruction through
13 continuous pupil-teacher interaction that includes teacher
14 modeling, guided practice, and independent practice.
15 (d) "Fluency" means the ability to read with speed, accuracy,
16 and proper expression.
17 (e) "Multi-tiered system of supports" means a comprehensive
18 framework that includes 3 distinct tiers of instructional support
19 and is composed of a collection of evidence-based strategies
20 designed to meet the individual needs and assets of a whole pupil
21 at all achievement levels.
22 (f) "Phonemic awareness" means the conscious awareness of all
23 of the following:
24 (i) Individual speech sounds, including, but not limited to,
25 consonants and vowels, in spoken syllables.
26 (ii) The ability to consciously manipulate through, including,
27 but not limited to, matching, blending, segmenting, deleting, or
28 substituting, individual speech sounds described in subparagraph
29 (i).
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

230
1 (iii) All levels of the speech sound system, including, but not
2 limited to, word boundaries, rhyme recognition, stress patterns,
3 syllables, onset-rime units, and phonemes.
4 (g) "Phonological" means relating to the system of contrastive
5 relationships among the speech sounds that constitute the
6 fundamental components of a language.
7 (h) "Progress monitoring" means the assessing of students'
8 academic performance, quantifying students' rates of improvement or
9 progress toward goals, and determining how students are responding
10 to instruction.
11 (i) "Rapid automatized naming (RAN)" means a task that
12 measures how quickly individuals can name objects; pictures;
13 colors; or symbols, including letters and digits, aloud, which can
14 predict later reading abilities for preliterate children.
15 Sec. 35d. (1) From the state school aid fund money
16 appropriated under section 11, for 2024-2025 2025-2026 only, there
17 is allocated an amount not to exceed $1,000,000.00 $2,500,000.00
18 for the department to provide grants to districts and intermediate
19 districts for the purchase of 1 or more components or trainings
20 through an eligible teacher training program for children with
21 dyslexia from a provider of an eligible teacher training program
22 for children with dyslexia as provided under this section.
23 (2) A provider that provides programming that meets all of the
24 following is considered to be a provider of an eligible teacher
25 training program for purposes of this section:
26 (a) Allows teachers to incorporate the 5 components essential
27 to an effective reading program into their daily lessons. The 5
28 components described in this subdivision are phonemic awareness,
29 phonics, vocabulary, fluency, and comprehension.
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

231
1 (b) Trains educators to teach reading using a proven,
2 multisensory approach.
3 (c) Educates teachers on how to explicitly and effectively
4 teach reading to beginning readers.
5 (d) Breaks reading and spelling down into smaller skills
6 involving letters and sounds, and then builds on these skills over
7 time.
8 (e) Uses multisensory teaching strategies to teach reading by
9 using sight, hearing, touch, and movement to help students connect
10 and learn the concepts being taught.
11 (3) Districts and intermediate districts may apply to the
12 department for grants to purchase components or training through an
13 eligible teacher training program from a provider of an eligible
14 teacher training program, and, upon receiving an application but
15 except as otherwise provided in this subsection, the department
16 shall make payments to districts and intermediate districts for
17 those purchases. The department shall make payments under this
18 section on a first-come, first-served basis until funds are
19 depleted.

15 Sec. 35m. (1) From the state school aid fund money
16 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated for 2024-2025 2025-
17 2026 only an amount not to exceed $87,000,000.00 $74,195,600.00 for
18 the purposes described in this section, including payments to
19 improve educational outcomes in literacy.
20 (2) The department shall create the committee for literacy
21 achievement that includes representatives of higher education and
22 early childhood literacy educators who have expertise in literacy
23 instruction and research. The department shall appoint the members
24 of the committee for literacy achievement. The department shall
25 develop a process to identify and assemble experts for the
26 committee. The primary focus of the committee is to recommend tools
27 and strategies with the goal of increasing student academic
28 outcomes in the area of literacy. The committee is encouraged to
29 collaborate with the Michigan Education Research Institute or other
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

235
1 entities with similar expertise on the subject of literacy. The
2 department shall determine the size, timeline, and benchmarks of
3 the committee. The committee shall do all of the following:
4 (a) Critically evaluate early literacy series and materials.
5 The committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using
6 the committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee
7 shall assign grade tiers to the series based on the series'
8 likelihood to increase student literacy outcomes. Priority must be
9 given to series that already have a demonstrated history of
10 increasing student outcomes.
11 (b) Critically evaluate literacy professional development. The
12 committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using the
13 committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee shall
14 assign grade tiers to the professional development based on the
15 professional development's likelihood to increase student literacy
16 outcomes. Priority must be given to professional development that
17 already has a demonstrated history of increasing student outcomes.
18 (c) Critically evaluate other applicable literacy tools or
19 services the committee determines to have a high likelihood or
20 demonstrated history of increasing student literacy outcomes.
21 (d) Create and maintain a rankings list of all early literacy
22 series and other items the committee has evaluated. The committee
23 shall denote on the list whether major changes have been made to an
24 early literacy series or other item on the list since the early
25 literacy series or other item was evaluated by the committee and if
26 the early literacy series or other item has not been evaluated
27 since the last ranking.
28 (3) An individual on the committee shall recuse themselves
29 from evaluating early literacy series and other items that the
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

236
1 individual has helped create, that the individual would benefit
2 from financially, or for which the individual has any other
3 conflict of interest.

5 shall make payments to districts and intermediate districts in an
6 amount and on a schedule determined by the department. The
7 department shall use the rankings list described in subsection (2)
8 as the basis for how funding is allocated in subsection (1) to
9 districts and intermediate districts. Districts and intermediate
10 districts using higher-ranked literacy tools that are proven to
11 increase student outcomes shall receive more funding than districts
12 and intermediate districts utilizing lower-ranked literacy tools in
13 both the base award and the per-pupil awards described in this
14 subsection. The department may determine that some districts and
15 intermediate districts will not receive funding under this section
16 based on the effectiveness of the early literacy series or other
17 items being utilized by the district or intermediate district. The
18 department shall award funding under this section using the
19 following methodology:
20 (a) A base award equal to the amount necessary for the
21 district or intermediate district to implement the chosen early
22 literacy series or other item. The department may place a cap on
23 the total award per district, intermediate district, or early
24 literacy series or other item and may choose not to award funding
25 if the early literacy series or other item is determined to be
26 ineffective or is determined to be less effective than other
27 choices.
28 (b) A per-pupil award based on the number of pupils in
29 membership at the district and intermediate district. The per-pupil
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

1 amount must be larger for districts and intermediate districts

13 faculty, or are courses taught in combination with online
14 instruction.
15 (6) The department shall distribute funds to eligible CTE
16 early middle college and CTE dual enrollment programs as follows:
17 (a) The department shall determine statewide average CTE costs
18 per pupil for each CIP code program by calculating statewide
19 average costs for each CIP code program for the 3 most recent
20 fiscal years.
21 (b) The distribution to each eligible CTE early middle college
22 or CTE dual enrollment program is the product of 50% of CTE costs
23 per pupil times the pupil enrollment of each eligible CTE early
24 middle college or CTE dual enrollment program in the immediately
25 preceding school year.
26 (7) To receive funds under this section, a CTE early middle
27 college or CTE dual enrollment program shall furnish to the
28 intermediate district that is the fiscal agent identified in
29 subsection (2), in a form and manner determined by the department,

1 all information needed to administer this program and meet federal
2 reporting requirements; shall allow the department or the

4 for which it receives funds; and shall reimburse the state for all
5 disallowances found in the review, as determined by the department.

2 Sec. 61v. (1) From the state school aid fund money
3 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated for 2025-2026 only
4 $50,000,000.00 for grants to districts and intermediate districts
5 to start or expand career and technical education programs.
6 (2) Districts and intermediate districts must apply on a
7 competitive basis for funding in a form and manner determined by
8 the department. All of the following apply to applications for
9 funding under this section:
10 (a) Applicants must demonstrate to the satisfaction of the
11 department that the district or intermediate district has a career
12 and technical education opportunity gap.
13 (b) Applicants must demonstrate the ability to continue
14 programs started with funding under this subsection in an ongoing
15 manner after funding received under this section is no longer
16 available.
17 (c) Applications must be scored on a tiered rating system with
18 criteria that must consider at least all of the following:
19 (i) The extent to which the applicant is able to receive
20 matching contributions from outside sources, including
21 philanthropic, business and industry contributions, and vocational
22 education millage revenue.
23 (ii) The magnitude to which the applicant demonstrates a career
24 and technical education opportunity gap.
25 (iii) Labor market demand for proposed programs.
26 (iv) Feasibility of planned implementation and evaluation
27 plans.
28 (3) All of the following apply to awards received by districts
29 and intermediate districts under this section:
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

283
1 (a) Awards must be used to create and sustain career and
2 technical education programs aligned with high-skill, high-wage,
3 and high-demand occupations across career clusters aligned with
4 local and regional labor market needs. Programs must be aligned
5 with state career and technical education standards, must integrate
6 stackable credentials, must create a program of study guided by a
7 program advisory committee, and must maintain all requirements of a
8 state-approved program.
9 (b) If funds are used for a middle school career and technical
10 education program, as determined by the department, the recipient
11 district or intermediate district must demonstrate how the program
12 will align academic content with practical career skills, integrate
13 the flexibilities of the Michigan merit curriculum in a program of
14 study from middle school through postsecondary, allow students to
15 earn high school academic and career and technical education
16 credits, and enable a more seamless transition into high school
17 career and technical education pathways.
18 (c) Districts and intermediate districts may utilize funding
19 for program startup costs, including developing and designing
20 programs of study, creating and furnishing labs for technical skill
21 training, hiring qualified staff, and other eligible purposes as
22 determined by the department.
23 (d) Awards shall be paid out over a period of 3 years. The
24 department shall work with each applicant to determine the most
25 effective level of funding in each year.

11 provided elsewhere in this article, and to provide technical
12 assistance to districts as authorized by the intermediate school
13 board.
14 (3) Intermediate districts receiving funds under this section
15 shall collaborate with the department to develop expanded
16 professional development opportunities for teachers to update and
17 expand their knowledge and skills needed to support the Michigan
18 merit curriculum.
19 (4) From the allocation in subsection (1), there is allocated
20 to an intermediate district, formed by the consolidation or
21 annexation of 2 or more intermediate districts or the attachment of
22 a total intermediate district to another intermediate district or
23 the annexation of all of the constituent K to 12 districts of a
24 previously existing intermediate district which has disorganized,
25 an additional allotment of $3,500.00 each fiscal year for each
26 intermediate district included in the new intermediate district for
27 3 years following consolidation, annexation, or attachment.
28 (5) To receive funding under this section, an intermediate
29 district shall do all of the following:
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

296
1 (a) Demonstrate to the satisfaction of the department that the
2 intermediate district employs at least 1 person who is trained in
3 pupil accounting and auditing procedures, rules, and regulations.

5 intermediate district employs at least 1 person who is trained in

12 (d) Furnish data and other information required by state and
13 federal law to the center and the department in the form and manner
14 specified by the center or the department, as applicable.
15 (e) Comply with section 1230g of the revised school code, MCL
16 380.1230g.

25 Sec. 94a. (1) There is created within the state budget office
26 in the department of technology, management, and budget the center
27 for educational performance and information. The center shall do
28 all of the following:
29 (a) Coordinate the collection of all data required by state
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

298
1 and federal law from districts, intermediate districts, and
2 postsecondary institutions.
3 (b) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's P-20
4 longitudinal data system and ensure that it meets the requirements
5 of subsection (4).
6 (c) Collect data in the most efficient manner possible to
7 reduce the administrative burden on reporting entities, including,
8 but not limited to, electronic transcript services.
9 (d) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's web-based
10 educational portal to provide information to school leaders,
11 teachers, researchers, and the public in compliance with all
12 federal and state privacy laws. Data must include, but are not
13 limited to, all of the following:
14 (i) Data sets that link teachers to student information,
15 allowing districts to assess individual teacher impact on student
16 performance and consider student growth factors in teacher and
17 principal evaluation systems.
18 (ii) Data access or, if practical, data sets, provided for
19 regional data hubs that, in combination with local data, can
20 improve teaching and learning in the classroom.
21 (iii) Research-ready data sets for researchers to perform
22 research that advances this state's educational performance.
23 (e) Provide data in a useful manner to allow state and local
24 policymakers to make informed policy decisions.
25 (f) Provide public reports to the residents of this state to
26 allow them to assess allocation of resources and the return on
27 their investment in the education system of this state.
28 (g) Other functions as assigned by the state budget director.
29 (2) Each state department, officer, or agency that collects
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

299
1 information from districts, intermediate districts, or

6 department of treasury under the uniform budgeting and accounting
7 act, 1968 PA 2, MCL 141.421 to 141.440a; the revised municipal

11 380.1351a.

14 (4) The center shall ensure that the P-20 longitudinal data

16 (a) Includes data at the individual student level from
17 preschool through postsecondary education and into the workforce.
18 (b) Supports interoperability by using standard data
19 structures, data formats, and data definitions to ensure linkage
20 and connectivity in a manner that facilitates the exchange of data
21 among agencies and institutions within the state and between
22 states.
23 (c) Enables the matching of individual teacher and student
24 records so that an individual student may be matched with those
25 teachers providing instruction to that student.
26 (d) Enables the matching of individual teachers with

28 prepared and recommended those teachers for state certification.
29 (e) Enables data to be easily generated for continuous
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

300
1 improvement and decision-making, including timely reporting to
2 parents, teachers, and school leaders on student achievement.

6 state reporting requirements.
7 (h) For data elements related to preschool through grade 12
8 and postsecondary, meets all of the following:
9 (i) Contains a unique statewide student identifier that does
10 not permit a student to be individually identified by users of the

12 (ii) Contains student-level enrollment, demographic, and
13 program participation information, including data associated with

19 115-404. Any reports or data access related specifically to tribal

21 federally recognized tribes in the state with the expectation that
22 the center, the department, and the tribes will work iteratively
23 toward meaningful reports, access, and use of these records to
24 improve shared education interests and outcomes.
25 (iii) Contains student-level information about the points at
26 which students exit, transfer in, transfer out, drop out, or
27 complete education programs.
28 (iv) Has the capacity to communicate with higher education data
29 systems.

1 (i) For data elements related to preschool through grade 12

6 (2) The Michigan Virtual University shall operate the Michigan
7 Virtual Learning Research Institute. The Michigan Virtual Learning
8 Research Institute shall do all of the following:
9 (a) Support and accelerate innovation in education through the
10 following activities:
11 (i) Test, evaluate, and recommend as appropriate new
12 technology-based instructional tools and resources.
13 (ii) Research, design, and recommend virtual education delivery
14 models for use by pupils and teachers that include age-appropriate
15 multimedia instructional content.
16 (iii) Research, develop, and recommend annually to the
17 department criteria by which cyber schools and virtual course
18 providers should be monitored and evaluated to ensure a quality
19 education for their pupils.
20 (iv) Based on pupil completion and performance data reported to
21 the department or the center from cyber schools and other virtual
22 course providers operating in this state, analyze the effectiveness
23 of virtual learning delivery models in preparing pupils to be
24 college- and career-ready and publish a report that highlights
25 enrollment totals, completion rates, and the overall impact on
26 pupils. The Michigan Virtual Learning Research Institute shall
27 submit the report to the house and senate appropriations
28 subcommittees on school aid, the state budget director, the house
29 and senate fiscal agencies, the department, districts, and
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

311
1 intermediate districts by not later than March 31 of each fiscal
2 year for which funding is allocated under this section.
3 (v) Provide an extensive professional development program to
4 at least 30,000 educational personnel, including teachers, school
5 administrators, and school board members, that focuses on the
6 effective integration of virtual learning into curricula and
7 instruction. The Michigan Virtual Learning Research Institute is
8 encouraged to work with the MiSTEM council described in section 99s
9 to coordinate professional development of teachers in applicable
10 fields. In addition, the Michigan Virtual Learning Research
11 Institute and external stakeholders are encouraged to coordinate
12 with the department for professional development in this state,

14 facilities, early childhood facilities, and after-school programs.

CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

312
1 inform learners how to file a complaint about course content and

313
1 learners.

CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

314
1 Virtual University, and make the results from these reviews
2 available to the public as part of the statewide catalog. The
3 Michigan Virtual Learning Research Institute shall ensure that the
4 statewide catalog is made available to the public on the Michigan
5 Virtual University website and shall allow the ability to link it
6 to each district's website as provided for in section 21f. The
7 statewide catalog must also contain all of the following:
8 (A) The number of enrollments in each virtual course in the
9 immediately preceding school year.
10 (B) The number of enrollments that earned 60% or more of the
11 total course points for each virtual course in the immediately
12 preceding school year.
13 (C) The pass rate for each virtual course.

20 recommendations publicly available.
21 (xiii) Provide a report on the activities of the Michigan
22 Virtual Learning Research Institute.

28 parameters:

5 (12) As used in this section:
6 (a) "Blended learning" means a hybrid instructional delivery
7 model where pupils are provided content, instruction, and
8 assessment, in part at a supervised educational facility away from
9 home where the pupil and a teacher with a valid Michigan teaching
10 certificate are in the same physical location and in part through
11 internet-connected learning environments with some degree of pupil
12 control over time, location, and pace of instruction.
13 (b) "Cyber school" means a full-time instructional program of
14 virtual courses for pupils that may or may not require attendance
15 at a physical school location.
16 (c) "Virtual course" means a course of study that is capable
17 of generating a credit or a grade and that is provided in an
18 interactive learning environment in which the majority of the
19 curriculum is delivered using the internet and in which pupils are
20 separated from their instructor or teacher of record by time or
21 location, or both.

CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

333
1 include, but are not limited to, salaries and benefits for staff,
2 vehicle purchases or leases, insurance, materials and equipment,

4 (3) To receive funding under this section, districts and
5 intermediate districts must apply in a form and manner prescribed
6 by the department. An applicant shall certify that the applicant
7 will run the program using district or intermediate district
8 employees and will not outsource any programming to an outside
9 third party.
10 (4) The maximum grant amount for an applicant is $500,000.00.
11 The department shall grant awards on a first-come, first-served
12 basis. If funding remains after 2025-2026, a district or
13 intermediate district that previously received a grant may receive
14 another grant as long as it continues the driver's training program
15 for another year, but no recipient may receive more than 1 grant in
16 a single fiscal year.

1 under section 11, there is allocated for 2024-2025 2025-2026 an

4 MiSTEM network may receive funds from private sources. If the
5 MiSTEM network receives funds from private sources, the MiSTEM
6 network shall expend those funds in alignment with the statewide
7 STEM strategy. Programs funded under this section are intended to
8 increase the number of pupils demonstrating proficiency in science

16 recommendations to the governor, the legislature, and the

18 the council for delivering STEM education-related opportunities to
19 pupils. The MiSTEM council shall use funds received under this
20 subsection to ensure that its members or their designees are
21 trained in the Change the Equation STEMworks rating system program
22 for the purpose of rating STEM programs.

24 recommendations for the funds allocated under subsection (4) by

27 council. All of the following apply:
28 (a) To be eligible for MiSTEM council approval as described in
29 this subsection, a program must satisfy all of the following:
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

339
1 (i) Align with this state's academic standards.
2 (ii) Have STEMworks certification.

4 programming, or educator professional learning experiences.
5 (iv) Focus predominantly on classroom-based STEM experiences or
6 professional learning experiences.

8 all network regions and include a diverse array of options for
9 students and educators and at least 1 program in each of the
10 following areas:
11 (i) Robotics.
12 (ii) Computer science or coding.

24 opportunities for pupils.

28 Sec. 101. (1) To be eligible to receive state aid under this
29 article, not later than the fifth Wednesday after the pupil

1 membership count day and not later than the fifth Wednesday after

5 the number of pupils enrolled and in regular daily attendance,
6 including identification of tuition-paying pupils, in the district
7 as of the pupil membership count day and as of the supplemental
8 count day, as applicable, for the current school year. In addition,
9 a district maintaining school during the entire year shall submit
10 and certify to the center and the intermediate superintendent, in
11 the form and manner prescribed by the center, the number of pupils
12 enrolled and in regular daily attendance in the district for the
13 current school year pursuant to rules promulgated by the
14 superintendent. Not later than the sixth Wednesday after the pupil
15 membership count day and not later than the sixth Wednesday after
16 the supplemental count day, the district shall resolve any pupil
17 membership conflicts with another district, correct any data
18 issues, and recertify the data in a form and manner prescribed by
19 the center and file the certified data with the intermediate

10 (10) A district may count up to 38 hours of professional

12 following apply to the counting of professional development as
13 pupil instruction under this subsection:

15 single day, that day may be counted as a day of pupil instruction.
16 (b) At least 8 hours of the professional development counted
17 as hours of pupil instruction under this subsection must be
18 recommended by a districtwide professional development advisory
19 committee appointed by the district board. The advisory committee
20 must be composed of teachers employed by the district who represent
21 a variety of grades and subject matter specializations, including
22 special education; nonteaching staff; parents; and administrators.
23 The majority membership of the committee must be composed of
24 teaching staff.

28 professional development providers that must include the Michigan
29 Virtual School.

362
1 (d) Professional development may only be counted as hours of

21 the last scheduled day of school for that school year.
22 (v) Not more than 10 hours of the professional development
23 takes place in a single month.

12 Sec. 104b. (1) In order to receive state aid under this
13 article, a district shall comply with this section and shall
14 administer the Michigan merit examination to pupils in grade 11,
15 and to pupils in grade 12 who did not take the complete Michigan
16 merit examination in grade 11, as provided in this section. The
17 Michigan merit examination consists of a college entrance test,
18 work skills test, and the summative assessment known as the
19 Michigan student test of educational progress (M-STEP).
20 (2) For the purposes of this section, the department of
21 technology, management, and budget shall contract with 1 or more
22 providers to develop, supply, and score the Michigan merit
23 examination. The Michigan merit examination shall consist of all of
24 the following:
25 (a) Assessment instruments that measure English language arts,
26 mathematics, reading, and science, and are used by the majority of
27 colleges and universities in this state for entrance purposes. This
28 may include 1 or more writing components. In selecting assessment
29 instruments to fulfill the requirements of this subdivision, the
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

366
1 department may consider the degree to which those assessment
2 instruments are aligned to this state's content standards.
3 (b) One or more tests from 1 or more test developers that
4 assess a pupil's ability to apply at least reading and mathematics
5 skills in a manner that is intended to allow employers to use the
6 results in making employment decisions. The department of
7 technology, management, and budget and the superintendent shall
8 ensure that any test or tests selected under this subdivision have
9 all the components necessary to allow a pupil to be eligible to
10 receive the results of a nationally recognized evaluation of
11 workforce readiness if the pupil's test performance is adequate.
12 (c) A social studies component.
13 (d) Any other component that is necessary to obtain the
14 approval of the United States Department of Education to use the
15 Michigan merit examination for the purposes of the no child left
16 behind act of 2001, Public Law 107-110, or the every student
17 succeeds act, Public Law 114-95.
18 (3) In addition to all other requirements of this section, all
19 of the following apply to the Michigan merit examination:
20 (a) The department of technology, management, and budget and
21 the superintendent shall ensure that any contractor used for
22 scoring the Michigan merit examination supplies an individual
23 report for each pupil that will identify for the pupil's parents
24 and teachers whether the pupil met expectations or failed to meet
25 expectations for each standard, to allow the pupil's parents and
26 teachers to assess and remedy problems before the pupil moves to
27 the next grade.
28 (b) The department of technology, management, and budget and
29 the superintendent shall ensure that any contractor used for
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

367
1 scoring, developing, or processing the Michigan merit examination
2 meets quality management standards commonly used in the assessment
3 industry, including at least meeting level 2 of the capability
4 maturity model developed by the Software Engineering Institute of
5 Carnegie Mellon University for the first year the Michigan merit
6 examination is offered to all grade 11 pupils and at least meeting
7 level 3 of the capability maturity model for subsequent years.
8 (c) The department of technology, management, and budget and
9 the superintendent shall ensure that any contract for scoring,
10 administering, or developing the Michigan merit examination
11 includes specific deadlines for all steps of the assessment
12 process, including, but not limited to, deadlines for the correct
13 testing materials to be supplied to schools and for the correct
14 results to be returned to schools, and includes penalties for
15 noncompliance with these deadlines.
16 (d) The superintendent shall ensure that the Michigan merit
17 examination meets all of the following:
18 (i) Is designed to test pupils on this state's content
19 standards in all subjects tested.
20 (ii) Complies with requirements of the no child left behind act
21 of 2001, Public Law 107-110 or the every student succeeds act,
22 Public Law 114-95.
23 (iii) Is consistent with the code of fair testing practices in
24 education prepared by the Joint Committee on Testing Practices of
25 the American Psychological Association.
26 (iv) Is factually accurate. If the superintendent determines
27 that a question is not factually accurate and should be excluded
28 from scoring, the state board and the superintendent shall ensure
29 that the question is excluded from scoring.
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

368
1 (4) A district shall include on each pupil's high school

3 (a) For each high school graduate who has completed the

11 providers of the Michigan merit examination to produce Michigan
12 merit examination subject area scores for each pupil participating
13 in the Michigan merit examination. To the extent that the
14 department determines that additional test items beyond those

21 nonpublic schools a simple and concise document that describes the
22 scoring for each subject area and indicates the scaled score ranges
23 for each subject area.
24 (6) The Michigan merit examination shall be administered in
25 each district during the last 12 weeks of the district's school
26 year. The superintendent shall ensure that the Michigan merit
27 examination is scored and the scores are returned to pupils, their
28 parents or legal guardians, and districts not later than the
29 beginning of the pupil's first semester of grade 12. The returned
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

369
1 scores shall indicate at least the pupil's scaled score for each
2 subject area component and the range of scaled scores for each
3 subject area. In reporting the scores to pupils, parents, and
4 schools, the superintendent shall provide standards-specific,
5 meaningful, and timely feedback on the pupil's performance on the
6 Michigan merit examination.
7 (7) A district shall administer the complete Michigan merit
8 examination to a pupil only once and shall not administer the
9 complete Michigan merit examination to the same pupil more than
10 once. If a pupil does not take the complete Michigan merit
11 examination in grade 11, the district shall administer the complete
12 Michigan merit examination to the pupil in grade 12. If a pupil
13 chooses to retake the college entrance examination component of the
14 Michigan merit examination, as described in subsection (2)(a), the
15 pupil may do so through the provider of the college entrance
16 examination component and the cost of the retake is the
17 responsibility of the pupil unless all of the following are met:
18 (a) The pupil has taken the complete Michigan merit
19 examination.
20 (b) The pupil meets the income eligibility criteria for free
21 breakfast, lunch, or milk, as determined under the Richard B.
22 Russell national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751 to 1769i.
23 (c) The pupil has applied to the provider of the college
24 entrance examination component for a scholarship or fee waiver to
25 cover the cost of the retake and that application has been denied.
26 (d) After taking the complete Michigan merit examination, the
27 pupil has not already received a free retake of the college
28 entrance examination component paid for either by this state or
29 through a scholarship or fee waiver by the provider.

370
1 (8) The superintendent shall ensure that the length of the
2 Michigan merit examination and the combined total time necessary to
3 administer all of the components of the Michigan merit examination
4 are the shortest possible that will still maintain the degree of
5 reliability and validity of the Michigan merit examination results
6 determined necessary by the superintendent. The superintendent
7 shall ensure that the maximum total combined length of time that
8 schools are required to set aside for pupils to answer all test
9 questions on the Michigan merit examination does not exceed 8 hours
10 if the superintendent determines that sufficient alignment to
11 applicable Michigan merit curriculum content standards can be
12 achieved within that time limit.
13 (9) A district shall provide accommodations to a pupil with
14 disabilities for the Michigan merit examination, as provided under
15 section 504 of title V of the rehabilitation act of 1973, 29 USC
16 794; subtitle A of title II of the Americans with disabilities act
17 of 1990, 42 USC 12131 to 12134; the individuals with disabilities
18 education act amendments of 1997, Public Law 105-17; and the
19 implementing regulations for those statutes. The provider or
20 providers of the Michigan merit examination and the superintendent
21 shall mutually agree upon the accommodations to be provided under
22 this subsection.
23 (10) To the greatest extent possible, the Michigan merit
24 examination shall be based on this state's content standards, as
25 appropriate. Annually, after each administration of the Michigan
26 merit examination, the department shall provide a report of the
27 points per standard so that teachers will know what content will be
28 covered within the Michigan merit examination. The department may
29 augment the college entrance and work skills components of the
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

371
1 Michigan merit examination to develop the assessment, depending on
2 the alignment of those components to this state's content
3 standards. If these components do not align to these standards, the
4 department shall produce additional components as required by law,
5 while minimizing the amount of time needed for assessments.
6 (11) A child who is a student in a nonpublic school or home
7 school may take the Michigan merit examination under this section.
8 To take the Michigan merit examination, a child who is a student in
9 a home school shall contact the district in which the child
10 resides, and that district shall administer the Michigan merit
11 examination, or the child may take the Michigan merit examination
12 at a nonpublic school if allowed by the nonpublic school. Upon
13 request from a nonpublic school, the superintendent shall direct
14 the provider or providers to supply the Michigan merit examination
15 to the nonpublic school and the nonpublic school may administer the
16 Michigan merit examination. If a district administers the Michigan
17 merit examination under this subsection to a child who is not
18 enrolled in the district, the scores for that child are not
19 considered for any purpose to be scores of a pupil of the district.
20 (12) In contracting under subsection (2), the department of
21 technology, management, and budget shall consider a contractor that
22 provides electronically-scored essays with the ability to score
23 constructed response feedback in multiple languages and provide
24 ongoing instruction and feedback.
25 (13) The purpose of the Michigan merit examination is to
26 assess pupil performance in mathematics, science, social studies,
27 and English language arts for the purpose of improving academic
28 achievement and establishing a statewide standard of competency.
29 The assessment under this section provides a common measure of data
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

372
1 that will contribute to the improvement of Michigan schools'
2 curriculum and instruction by encouraging alignment with Michigan's
3 curriculum framework standards and promotes pupil participation in
4 higher level mathematics, science, social studies, and English
5 language arts courses. These standards are based upon the
6 expectations of what pupils should learn through high school and
7 are aligned with national standards.
8 (14) For a pupil enrolled in a middle college program, other

22 or other program created under a memorandum of understanding, that
23 allows a pupil to graduate from high school with both a high school
24 diploma and a certificate or degree from a community college or

18 (b) "Career pathway" means a combination of rigorous and high-
19 quality education, training, and other services that comply with
20 all of the following:

22 this state or in the regional economy involved.

25 apprenticeships registered under the act of August 16, 1937,
26 commonly referred to as the national apprenticeship act, 29 USC 50

388
1 (iv) Includes, as appropriate, education offered concurrently
2 with and in the same context as workforce preparation activities
3 and training for a specific occupation or occupational cluster.

5 the particular needs of an individual in a manner that accelerates
6 the educational and career advancement of the individual to the
7 extent practicable.
8 (vi) Enables an individual to attain a secondary school diploma
9 or its recognized equivalent, and at least 1 recognized
10 postsecondary credential.

12 occupation or occupational cluster.

23 residence an amount for tuition that does not exceed the tuition
24 rate computed under section 1401 of the revised school code, MCL
25 380.1401. The rate charged by a district shall be uniform within
26 each category of tuition pupils enrolled in the district. However,
27 for a tuition pupil who resides in a K-5, K-6, or K-8 district and
28 who is enrolled in a grade not offered by the pupil's district of
29 residence, the tuition rate charged to the pupil's district of
CRM S01533'25 (S-3) 6ogd4e

389
1 residence shall not exceed the foundation allowance of the pupil's
2 district of residence or the foundation allowance of the educating
3 district, whichever is greater. A district receiving funds under
4 this act shall not charge tuition to a parent or custodian of a
5 pupil enrolled in the district as a nonresident pupil.

7 public school employees' retirement system pursuant to the public

9 to 38.1437, is made using the individual projected benefit entry
10 age normal cost method of valuation and risk assumptions adopted by
